conbody.p: Liminality and boundaries are central themes of Halloween, reflecting moments and spaces where ordinary rules are suspended and transitions occur. Halloween traditions frequently focus on thresholds between opposing states, such as life and death, safety and danger, or the familiar and the unknown.

section.title: Cultural and Conceptual Background
section.p: The concept of liminality describes transitional states in which individuals or spaces exist between defined categories. Historically, seasonal festivals associated with Halloween marked a transition between harvest and winter, reinforcing the idea of a world temporarily out of balance.
Boundaries—such as doorways, borders, and nighttime hours—feature prominently in folklore as points where supernatural encounters are believed to occur.

section.title: Symbolic Meaning
section.p: Symbolically, liminality represents uncertainty, transformation, and vulnerability. Boundaries define where one state ends and another begins, making them powerful sites for symbolic tension.
In Halloween contexts, these boundaries are often portrayed as fragile or permeable, allowing supernatural elements to cross into the ordinary world.

section.title: Liminality in Modern Halloween Traditions
section.p: Modern Halloween practices emphasize boundary-crossing through activities such as trick-or-treating, in which participants move between private and public spaces, or haunted attractions that invite people to cross into controlled environments of fear.
Visual imagery such as gates, graveyards, and fog-covered paths reinforces the theme by highlighting transitional spaces rather than fixed destinations.

example.title: Halloween Elements That Reflect Liminality and Boundaries

ul.li: Doorways, gates, and thresholds featured in decorations
Graveyards as spaces between life and death
Nighttime settings that blur ordinary social rules
